article_title: Julia 1.13 Reaches Release Candidate With Mutually Recursive Types, a flisp-Free Constructor Path, and Unicode 17
summary: Approved after manual source verification. The automated 'orphan URL' error is a false positive: the Wikipedia URL contains a literal ')' that the link-extraction regex truncates, but the full URL https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julia_(programming_language) IS in article.sources and has a committed snapshot (source-4, HTTP 200). All 5 sources read; every quote and version string verified verbatim.

findings.message: Body cites 1 URL(s) not in article.sources (provenance break)
findings.details:
These URLs appear as Markdown links in body_markdown but are missing from article.sources, so they have no committed snapshot under sources/YYYY-MM/<slug>/ and cannot be verified through the provenance chain. Orphan URL(s):
  - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julia_(programming_language

content_preview.title: Julia 1.13 Reaches Release Candidate With Mutually Recursive Types, a flisp-Free Constructor Path, and Unicode 17
content_preview.summary: Julia 1.13 hit its first release candidate on April 29, adding mutually recursive types and moving default constructor generation out of the language's Scheme bootstrap layer.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The Julia programming language has reached the release-candidate stage for its next minor version. The first release candidate, tagged `v1.13.0-rc1`, was published on April 29 and is described by its maintainers as the "First release candidate for the upcoming 1.13.0 release," according to the [GitHub release notes](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/releases/tag/v1.13.0-rc1). The candidate caps a beta cycle that began with `v1.13.0-beta1` on January 13, per the project's [GitHub re...

recommendations: Consider adding julialang.org (the official Julia project blog, a primary source) to config/source_allowlist.txt in a future maintenance pass — not required for this approval.

editor_notes.content_quality: Clean, well-structured News piece (669 words). Overview/What We Know/What We Don't Know/Analysis structure is appropriate. Technical depth is accurate and does not overreach.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 source snapshots read from disk (sources/2026-06/julia-113-.../source-0..4.html.gz), all HTTP 200. source-0 (GitHub releases page): confirms v1.12.6 is the latest stable (dated 2026-04-10), v1.13.0-beta1 dated 2026-01-13, and shows NO stable v1.13.0 tag (only -beta1/2/3 and -rc1). source-1 (v1.13.0-rc1 release tag): confirms the rc1 tag, the 2026-04-29 date, the verbatim 'First release candidate for the upcoming 1.13.0 release' quote, and the NEWS.md pointer 'for what will be new in 1.13'. source-2 (NEWS.md, release-1.13 branch): all feature specifics verified verbatim — @__FUNCTION__ 'to refer to the innermost enclosing function', 'Support for Unicode 17', the hash(::AbstractString) change including the 'zero-copy / zero-cost function' and 'may need to be deleted' wording, AbstractSpinLock with 'SpinLock <: AbstractSpinLock', PaddedSpinLock 'has extra padding to avoid false sharing', nth 'n-th element of a generic iterable', and --trace-eval 'expressions being evaluated during top-level evaluation'. source-3 (julialang.org This Month in Julia World): mutually-recursive-types and flisp/constructor quotes verified verbatim, including 'Default constructor generation has been moved from C/flisp to Julia' and 'preparatory work for a flisp-free bootstrap using JuliaLowering'. source-4 (Wikipedia): confirms Julia 1.0 released 8 August 2018, multiple dispatch, dynamically typed.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims trace to cited sources. No fabricated PR numbers or dates. The two automated findings were adjudicated: (1) julialang.org not in allowlist is informational — it is the official Julia project blog (primary source). (2) The 'orphan URL provenance break' is a FALSE POSITIVE: the body link target https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julia_(programming_language) contains a literal ')' that the script's Markdown-link regex truncates to '...(programming_language', producing a phantom mismatch. The full, correct URL appears in article.sources and has a committed, verified snapshot (source-4). The provenance chain is intact.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism; appropriately hedged ('What We Don't Know' section explicitly flags that rc1 had not been promoted to stable).
editor_notes.originality: Original — no prior Julia coverage in the archive. Adjacent programming-language releases (Rust, Go, TypeScript, Python) are covered but no overlap with this story.
editor_notes.framing_check: RC-vs-stable framing is correct. Headline says 'Reaches Release Candidate' (not 'ships'/'released stable'). Body states v1.13.0-rc1 'had not yet been promoted to a final release' and that the releases page shows v1.12.6 as the latest stable with no stable v1.13.0 tag. This matches the sources exactly.
recommendations: Optionally allowlist julialang.org in a future maintenance pass; not a blocker.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully-sourced News submission. The automated REJECT was driven solely by a URL-regex false positive; manual verification of all 5 snapshots confirms every claim, quote, and version string. Verdict overridden to APPROVE.
